issues.title: Parallelize the amplification EVENT loop (amplification.rs:86-209) — distinct from #178's follower-fetch loop

comments.content:
SCOPE: src/pipeline/amplification.rs:86-209 — the "Store each event in the database" loop. `for event in &events` awaits three things per iteration: posts::fetch_post_text at :103 (one Bluesky round-trip), nli.score_pair at :133, and db.insert_amplification_event at :189. Strictly one event at a time.

NOT THE SAME AS #178 (closed). #178 fixed the amplifier FOLLOWER-FETCH loop, landed as 1d367e1 "perf(amplification): parallelize amplifier follower fetch" — that is the code now at amplification.rs:361, which does use buffer_unordered. #178 title said "Analogous serial loop in amplification.rs" which makes it LOOK covered; it is not. The event loop at :86-209 was never touched. NOT THE SAME AS #189 either — that is the finalize embed_batch move plus constellation/client.rs + web/scan_job.rs loops.

MEASURED COST, from Railway staging deploy logs for the 2026-07-18 scan (28m24s total, 473 accounts, degraded=true):
  02:53:28.464  "Processing amplification events total_events=359"
  02:58:07.917  "Skipping follower analysis for reposts/likes skipped=353"
  => 4m39s for 359 events = ~0.78s/event, ~16% of total scan wall-clock.
Contrast the follower fetches immediately after, which #178 already parallelized: 02:58:07.970 -> 02:58:08.004, i.e. 6 accounts in ~0.1s. That is the shape this loop should have.

APPROACH: reuse the pattern already present in this same file at amplification.rs:342-364 — futures::stream::iter(...).map(...).buffer_unordered(concurrency.clamp(1,64)) followed by sort_by_key to restore deterministic order. sweep.rs:95-118 is the other reference implementation.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Concurrency 4-8, NOT 64. PublicAtpClient still has no 429/backoff (#182). If 429s show up in staging logs after this ships, do #182 before raising it.
- MUST preserve determinism. Two separate concerns: (a) DB insert order / the amplification_events rows, and (b) the println! evidence output at :198-207. buffer_unordered yields in completion order, so the results must be collected and re-sorted into the original `events` order before inserting/printing.
- Scores must be identical to the serial version. fetch_post_text + score_with_context + score_pair are per-event pure w.r.t. each other, so parallelizing must not change any value — only ordering, which the sort restores.
- Watch db.insert_amplification_event: keep the inserts sequential after the sort, or confirm the Database impl is safe to call concurrently (Postgres pool is fine; SqliteDatabase serializes on a Mutex anyway).

EXPECTED: 4m39s -> well under 1m at concurrency 8, i.e. ~15% off total scan time, with no schema change and no risk of shifting scores.

Relates: #178 (closed, different loop), #182 (429 backoff — gates concurrency), #189 (other speedups), #184 (amplification resumability).

CORRECTION — my original cost attribution in this issue was WRONG, found while doing the TDD understand-first pass. Do not implement against the numbers above.

WHAT I GOT WRONG: I claimed 4m39s for the event loop at ~0.78s/event, driven by a serial fetch_post_text per event. Two errors.

(1) The loop is 2m16s, not 4m39s. I had used the wrong end boundary. The 359 per-event println lines (":198-207") are in the Railway log stream; anchoring each to its nearest preceding timestamped line shows the LAST event emits at 02:55:44.38, not 02:58:07.92. So:
  event loop      02:53:28.46 -> 02:55:44.38  = 2m16s  (~8% of the 28m24s run)
  post-loop work  02:55:44.38 -> 02:58:07.92  = 2m23s  (Phase B setup: scorable_events filter + unique_amplifiers build — SEPARATE problem, not this issue)

(2) fetch_post_text is NOT the bottleneck. The fetch at :103 is gated on `event_type == "quote" || event_type == "reply"`. The run logged "Skipping follower analysis for reposts/likes skipped=353" out of total_events=359, so only ~6 events ever call it. Parallelizing that fetch across 6 events saves seconds, not minutes.

WHAT ACTUALLY DOMINATES: sequential DB write round-trips. Anchored event timings are uniform at ~0.4s/event across the whole loop — 59 events in the first 25.1s (0.43s/ea), then 299 events in the next 110.8s (0.37s/ea). Uniform cost across events that mostly do NOTHING except db.insert_amplification_event means the insert IS the cost. 359 x ~0.4s = ~144s, matching the observed 2m16s. insert_amplification_event (postgres.rs:438-469) is a single INSERT ... RETURNING id — one round-trip, called 359 times strictly sequentially.

Note ~400ms per round-trip is anomalously high for Railway internal networking (expect low single-digit ms). Worth a separate look; it may indicate connection churn or a cross-region hop. But the structural finding holds regardless of the per-op constant: 359 sequential round-trips.

THIS ALSO PARTLY REVERSES what I told Bryan on #189. I said DB round-trips were a rounding error. That is true for FINALIZE (which is ONNX-bound, median 1.56s/account). It is NOT true for this phase — the amplification event loop is DB-write-bound.

REVISED OPTIONS (needs a decision before implementing):
(A) Parallelize the whole loop body incl. inserts via buffer_unordered(8). ~144s -> ~18s. Matches this issue as scoped. COST: insert order becomes nondeterministic, so amplification_events serial `id` assignment no longer follows event order. Need to confirm nothing depends on id ordering (detected_at is a DB default and pile-on keys off timestamps, so probably fine). The println evidence output can still be made deterministic by collecting + sort_by_key before printing.
(B) Batch the inserts into one multi-row INSERT / UNNEST. ~144s -> ~1 round-trip. Strictly better on both speed and determinism, but bigger: new Database trait method plus SqliteDatabase and PgDatabase impls.
(C) Both: batch inserts, and parallelize the 6 fetches (marginal).

RECOMMEND (B) on the merits — it is the actual fix, keeps ordering deterministic, and removes the round-trips rather than hiding them. (A) is smaller and matches the approved scope. Paused for Bryan to choose.

TOOLING CORRECTION (from Bryan). Verified what the superpowers plugin actually ships, v6.0.3 at ~/.claude/plugins/cache/superpowers-marketplace/superpowers/6.0.3:
- NO commands/ directory exists. package.json declares only {"pi": {"skills": ["./skills"]}}. The plugin exposes 14 SKILLS and zero slash commands, so there is no /superpowers:* command to run in this or any session.
- The two skills I used (writing-plans, test-driven-development) did load from that real plugin path, so the loop was not improvised — but it used the wrong ENTRY POINT for this repo.
- This repo established loop is superpowers:subagent-driven-development: 66 artifacts in .superpowers/sdd/ incl. progress.md, which currently holds the #186 batch-classifier ledger (5 tasks, commits 8cac810..a9824e5). That is the shape #192 work should follow. Note .superpowers/ is gitignored (.gitignore:50) so the ledger is local-only.

CONSEQUENCE FOR THIS ISSUE: subagent-driven-development takes a WRITTEN PLAN of discrete tasks as its input, and the skill explicitly instructs "do not pause to check in between tasks — execute all tasks without stopping." So the design decision has to be made BEFORE the loop starts, not during it. Still blocked on the A/B/C choice recorded in the previous comment (recommend B: batch the inserts). Once chosen I will write docs/superpowers/plans/2026-07-18-amplification-event-batching-impl.md, then run subagent-driven-development against it with a fresh .superpowers/sdd/progress.md.

DECISION: Bryan chose option (B) — batch the inserts into a single multi-row INSERT via a new Database trait method, keeping ordering deterministic. Options A and C are rejected.

CORRECTED PROBLEM STATEMENT (supersedes this issue title and my first comment): the amplification event loop is DB-WRITE-BOUND, not network-bound. The title says "parallelize"; the actual fix is "batch". Measured: the loop runs 02:53:28.46 -> 02:55:44.38 = 2m16s for 359 events at a uniform ~0.4s/event, and only ~6 of those events call fetch_post_text (it is gated on quote/reply; the run logged skipped=353). db.insert_amplification_event (amplification.rs:178) is one INSERT..RETURNING id round-trip per event, 359 of them strictly sequential. 359 x ~0.4s = ~144s, matching the observed 2m16s.

IMPLEMENTATION FACTS gathered for the plan:
- Trait decl: src/db/traits.rs:81-92, returns Result<i64>.
- Postgres impl: src/db/postgres.rs:438-469, single INSERT..RETURNING id via sqlx.
- SQLite impl: src/db/sqlite.rs:112-137, delegates to src/db/queries.rs:294-324 (conn.execute + last_insert_rowid).
- ONLY production caller is src/pipeline/amplification.rs:178, and it DISCARDS the returned id (`.await?;`). So the batch method does not need to return ids — this materially simplifies the API and avoids RETURNING-order questions.
- Existing trait tests: tests/unit_labels.rs:623-676 (SQLite, in-memory via test_db(), TEST_USER const), tests/db_postgres.rs:168+ (gated on DATABASE_URL).
- Not to be touched: insert_amplification_event_raw (traits.rs:116) is migrate-only and preserves detected_at.

DETERMINISM REQUIREMENT: a single multi-row INSERT assigns serial ids in VALUES order, so batching is strictly MORE deterministic than the current per-row loop, not less. The println evidence output at amplification.rs:198-207 must continue to emit in original event order.

PROCESS: Bryan approves spec/plan before implementation. Writing the plan now via superpowers:writing-plans into docs/superpowers/plans/; will STOP for approval before running subagent-driven-development. Branch feat/parallel-amplification-events off staging, clean at e72b681.

PLAN WRITTEN, awaiting Bryan approval before any implementation. docs/superpowers/plans/2026-07-18-batch-amplification-event-inserts.md on branch feat/parallel-amplification-events.

5 tasks: (1) NewAmplificationEvent model + trait method + compiling stubs; (2) SQLite chunked multi-row insert in queries.rs; (3) Postgres UNNEST impl; (4) two-pass restructure of the event loop; (5) changelog. Tasks 1+2 share a commit because T1 stub forward-references the T2 function.

Behavior-preservation contract baked in as 5 explicit acceptance criteria, each mapped to a test in the self-review: same rows in same id order, same values, same stdout, same return value, empty-slice no-op. Ordering tests assert on id rather than detected_at because a batch shares one DB-stamped timestamp. Chunk-boundary test uses 250 rows across 3 chunks to catch placeholder base-index bugs.

Deliberate scope call recorded in the plan: pass 1 of the loop stays SEQUENTIAL. Only ~6 of 359 events do network work, so parallelizing buys almost nothing and would reorder the println evidence output. Batching the writes is the whole win. NO subagent-driven-development run yet — human gate first, per Bryan process note.
Tasks 1+2 complete: NewAmplificationEvent + trait method + SQLite chunked multi-row insert. 3 tests green incl. a 250-row chunk-boundary test. Postgres still on the temporary per-row loop stub.

SDD Task 1+2 complete (commit 4469d44). SPEC ✅ / QUALITY Approved, no Critical findings. Reviewer mutation-tested the SQLite chunk arithmetic: a stride off-by-one and a count-preserving value smear were both caught by the 250-row test. Controller independently re-verified 3/3 batch tests + cargo check clean on web and postgres (IDE diagnostics claiming E0046/E0425 were stale pre-index, same as the #186 precedent).

TWO REVIEW FINDINGS CARRIED FORWARD:
1. ERROR-PATH BEHAVIOR CHANGE (needs Bryan visibility). Batching makes the write all-or-nothing; the old per-event loop kept events 0..k on a mid-loop crash. CLAUDE.md lists "crash-resilient pipelines (incremental DB writes)" as a deliberate property, and the plan contract was silent on the error path. DECIDED: accept all-or-nothing, because per #184 a resumed scan re-runs the ENTIRE amplification phase from scratch — partially-persisted events are never resumed from, so they would be re-collected and re-inserted as DUPLICATES. All-or-nothing is strictly better for idempotency here. Will be documented in the Task 4 code comment and flagged in the final report.
2. POSTGRES TEST PARITY. The contract is currently asserted for SQLite only, but prod runs Postgres, and Task 3 replaces the trivial per-row stub with UNNEST where column-order and ordering bugs are plausible. Task 3 dispatch extended beyond the plan to port all three tests (id ascent, empty no-op, multi-row fidelity) to tests/db_postgres.rs.

Task 3 complete: Postgres UNNEST impl replaces the per-row stub. One round-trip for any batch size. Parity + empty-slice + 250-row column-fidelity tests green against local Postgres (12/12 db_postgres.rs, 117/117 full postgres suite). Clippy clean web/default/postgres. Commit bfeb40b.

issues.title: Scan CPU speedup: move topic embedding out of serial finalize + parallelize residual discovery loops

comments.content:
Successor to #178 (closed — its sweep/amplification parallelization already shipped in cd6ae9c/1d367e1/dd07ad5). This is where the remaining onboarding-scan wall-clock actually is. Full plan: docs/superpowers/plans/2026-07-18-scan-speedup-and-hosting.md

CONTEXT — the scan is NOT core-starved, so this is a scheduling problem, not a hosting one. All three ONNX sessions are Arc<Mutex<Session>> (toxicity/onnx.rs:45, topics/embeddings.rs:31, scoring/nli.rs:100), so only one inference per model runs process-wide regardless of gather concurrency=8. ort already parallelizes intra-op across cores (no with_intra_threads override anywhere), so a bigger box gives sub-linear returns. Confirms the infra-spike conclusion quoted in #188: I/O-bound, not CPU-bound.

TASK 1 (biggest win, LOW risk) — move the topic-overlap embedding out of finalize into gather. profile.rs:458 runs embed_batch over up to 50 posts per account, inside the SERIAL run_finalize loop (scan_phases/mod.rs:430), holding the global session mutex. 2900 accounts x ~1.5s = ~72min, which matches the measured ~74min finalize exactly. Gather is I/O-bound at concurrency 8 with idle CPU and ALREADY runs ONNX there (the clean-pass at gather.rs:232), so the inference hides behind network waits. Compute the 384-dim target mean in gather, stash it on the AccountInput blob, and let finalize do a cosine dot product in microseconds. Requires bumping ACCOUNT_INPUT_SCHEMA_VERSION; the version-mismatch path at finalize.rs:96-105 already clears staging and re-gathers, so an in-flight deploy self-heals. WATCH OUT: fingerprint_posts is selected from the Stage-2 sample at profile.rs:358-369 — reproduce that selection EXACTLY in gather or every accounts overlap score shifts silently. Gate with an equivalence test. Expected win ~60-70min.

TASK 2 (LOW-MED risk) — parallelize the three residual serial discovery loops. Verified today that constellation/client.rs and web/scan_job.rs contain NO buffer_unordered: constellation/client.rs:100-155 find_amplification_events does 2 awaited get_backlinks per URI (~100 serial calls for 50 posts); constellation/client.rs:171-193 find_likers (~50); web/scan_job.rs:511-539 loops fetch_replies_to_post per post (~50). ~200 strictly sequential HTTP round-trips before the pipeline starts. Copy the proven in-repo pattern at sweep.rs:95-118 (stream::iter().map().buffer_unordered(n) + sort_by_key to restore deterministic order). Start at concurrency 4-8, NOT 64 — Constellation has no documented rate limit and PublicAtpClient still has no backoff (#182). If 429s appear in staging logs after this ships, do #182 before going further.

TASK 3 (MEDIUM risk, gated) — batch the 5 NLI hypotheses into one forward pass (scoring/nli.rs:227-237 currently does 5 sequential single-item runs, each its own spawn_blocking + mutex acquire). HARD GATE: the comment at nli.rs:181-184 warns this export SEGFAULTS rather than errors on unexpected input shapes. Verify a [5, max_len] batch is accepted before writing any code; if it segfaults, close as not-viable rather than working around it. ~5x on all NLI.

TASK 4 (MEDIUM risk, gated) — collapse the Stage-1/Stage-2 double fetch. gather.rs:150 fetches 25 posts, :173 re-fetches 50 for every proceeding account; both are single-page getAuthorFeed calls (page_size = max_posts.min(100), bluesky/posts.rs:232) so 50 costs the same as 25. GATE: measure the Stage-1 terminal rate first — if most accounts early-exit at Stage 1 this inverts the economics, because those accounts currently pay only the cheap fetch.

TASK 5 (LOW risk) — kill the is_score_stale N+1 at sweep.rs:130-134 and amplification.rs:383 (one serial DB round-trip per candidate). The topic-first path at sweep.rs:206 already does it correctly: pull the fresh-DID set once, test in memory. Must apply the same 7-day cutoff. ~9s at 2900 candidates, 60s+ on a 20k second-degree pool.

DEFERRED / noted, not scheduled: ONNX session pool (the deepest structural limit — tasks above route around it, they do not remove it; measure before building, ort already uses the cores); AuditWriter::from_env constructed per NLI pair (profile.rs:517, amplification.rs:153) = a file open syscall per pair; per-row DB writes in enqueue_classifications (postgres.rs:1082) and record_classification_verdicts (:1172) — one round-trip per row inside a transaction, fixable with UNNEST/push_values; PgPool created with no PgPoolOptions (postgres.rs:37) so max_connections defaults to 10, a latent ceiling if concurrency rises.

MEASURED PHASE BREAKDOWN of the 2026-07-18 staging run (28m24s, 473 accounts, degraded). Source: Railway staging deploy logs, phase-boundary lines from charcoal::pipeline::scan_phases. This replaces the unverified 2900-account baseline.

  amplification  02:52:49.996 -> 02:59:59.298  =  7m09s  (25%)
  gather         02:59:59.298 -> 03:08:50.716  =  8m51s  (31%)   candidates=474
  burst          03:08:50.716 -> 03:12:23.488  =  3m33s  (12%)   pending=751, errored=0
  finalize       03:12:24.777 -> 03:21:14.304  =  8m50s  (31%)   accounts=340

WHAT KIND OF I/O — the run is ~68% NETWORK I/O and ~31% ONNX CPU. Postgres round-trips are a rounding error, NOT the crux for this workload:
- gather = Bluesky getAuthorFeed, 813 of 1381 log lines in the phase are charcoal::bluesky::posts.
- burst = RunPod queue wait, not compute. classifier_runpod_timing shows delay_time_ms climbing 414 -> 2497 -> 4573 -> 5651 -> 7734 while execution_time_ms stays 400-1956ms. Queue-bound, exactly the #186 batching thesis. Only ~27 texts actually reached Stage 2 (80 metric lines / 3 per call) out of 751 pending — the ONNX clean-pass filtered the rest.
- finalize = ONNX embedding, NOT DB. 352 accounts, median gap 1.56s, p90 1.61s, max 1.98s, mean 1.50s. That distribution is far too tight for network or DB (which jitter); it is deterministic CPU. It also matches the 1.5s/account from the 2900-account run, so the per-account cost is stable even though the totals differ.
- DB math: finalize does 3 queries/account x 340 = ~1020 round-trips. At Railway networked Postgres RTT that is single-digit seconds out of 28 minutes.

AMPLIFICATION DECOMPOSES FURTHER (only 19 log lines in 7 minutes — it is blocking, not busy):
  02:52:50 -> 02:53:28   38s   setup (Constellation likes=282, graph distances=191)
  02:53:28 -> 02:58:07   4m39s SERIAL event loop over total_events=359 = ~0.78s/event
  02:58:07 -> 02:58:08   0.1s  6 follower fetches, fully concurrent — #178 working as intended
  02:58:08 -> 02:59:59   1m51s UNLOGGED gap before "entering gather candidates=474"

REVISED PRIORITY — easiest high-leverage first:
1. Parallelize the serial amplification event loop (amplification.rs:86-209). ~4m39s = 16% of the run, one network fetch_post_text per event done one at a time. LOWEST EFFORT of any fix here: the exact buffer_unordered + sort_by_key pattern already exists 250 lines below it at amplification.rs:361. No schema change, no version bump, no risk of silently shifting scores. Keep concurrency 4-8 because #182 (no 429 backoff) is still open.
2. The 1m51s unlogged gap. INFERRED, not proven: the most likely cause is the per-candidate is_score_stale N+1 at amplification.rs:383 over 474 candidates. Cheap to confirm by timing that loop before fixing it. Fix is the same bulk-set pattern already used at sweep.rs:206. ~6.5%.
3. THEN Task 1 (move embed_batch out of finalize into gather). Biggest single win at 8m50s = 31%, now confirmed by measurement, but higher effort and it is the one change that can silently shift every overlap score.

1+2 together are ~6m30s = 23% for an afternoon of low-risk work using patterns already proven in these same files. 1+2+3 is ~15m20s = 54%, which would take this run from 28m24s to roughly 13m.

NOTE: parallelizing finalize instead of moving the embedding would NOT work — all three ONNX sessions are behind a global Arc<Mutex<Session>>, so concurrent embed_batch calls would just queue on the mutex.

PGBOUNCER (railway one-click, docs.railway.com/databases/postgresql-pgbouncer) — RECOMMEND AGAINST, for three independent reasons:
- Does not help scan speed. Per the breakdown above, DB round-trips are a few seconds of a 28-minute run. PgBouncer multiplexes connections; it does not make queries faster.
- Does not help memory. Charcoal caps at sqlx default max_connections=10 (postgres.rs:37, bare PgPool::connect with no PgPoolOptions). PgBouncer reduces Postgres-side backend count; with <=10 backends there is nothing to reclaim. Measured prod Postgres RAM is flat at 0.0365GB avg / 0.0382GB max. The memory spike is in charcoal-web, not Postgres: staging charcoal-web max 1.4391GB vs 0.0815GB avg, coinciding with scans. That is the three resident ONNX models (~300MB) plus in-flight scan data (751-row queue, per-account samples).
- It is actively risky. Railway pooled DATABASE_URL is TRANSACTION mode, and Railway own docs state session-level features including advisory locks are unsupported. run_migrations takes pg_advisory_lock on a dedicated connection (postgres.rs:76-83, released :167). Charcoal reads only DATABASE_URL (config.rs:94) and has no unpooled path, so enabling PgBouncer would point migrations at the pooled URL and break that lock. Separately, sqlx prepared-statement caching is a known transaction-mode incompatibility. Adopting it would require code changes to honor DATABASE_UNPOOLED_URL.
- Cost: the docs do not state billing explicitly, but the PgBouncer Overview panel scales replicas 1-6, i.e. it is a deployable component with its own replicas. On Railway per-resource metering that means new metered vCPU/RAM. Small, but nonzero, for zero benefit here.
Revisit only if concurrent CLIENT connections ever become the constraint (many app replicas). At max_connections=10 that is far off.

issues.title: Evaluate flat-rate hosting alternatives to Railway usage-based billing

comments.content:
TRIGGER: Railway bill for May 2026 was $30.32 (Pro $20/mo subscription includes $20 usage, then metered above it on vCPU-time, RAM-GB-time, volume $/GB, egress). Bryan: "it is already time to look at different options, either a VPS that does not charge on usage or something."

SCOPE: evaluate flat-rate / fixed-price hosting alternatives to Railways usage-based model for the charcoal-web service (Axum API + SvelteKit SPA) + Postgres (pgvector) + a persistent volume for ONNX models (/data/models). Two environments today: prod (charcoal.watch <- main) and staging (<- staging), each with its own Postgres + volume.

CANDIDATES TO PRICE OUT (flat monthly, no per-usage metering): Hetzner Cloud / dedicated (note: soot v2 spec already assumes a Hetzner box ~$80/mo fixed), a generic VPS (DigitalOcean/Linode/Vultr fixed droplet), Fly.io, bare metal. Compare against current Railway ~$30/mo trajectory.

KEY REQUIREMENTS the target must satisfy:
- Postgres 16+ with pgvector (384-dim embeddings) — self-managed on the box or a managed flat-rate add-on.
- Persistent volume for ONNX models (~216MB toxicity+embeddings) + the NLI model (~87MB); models auto-download on first scan.
- Docker deploy (Dockerfile already exists: Ubuntu 24.04, Rust 1.94 + Node 22, glibc 2.39 for ort-sys). Railway builder is DOCKERFILE not Railpack.
- Custom domain + TLS for charcoal.watch (Porkbun DNS, currently ALIAS to Railway).
- Two envs (prod + staging) — a single bigger box could host both via containers/compose.
- Outbound bandwidth: scans are I/O-heavy against Bluesky public API + Constellation + RunPod; check egress is not separately metered/capped.

NOTE — this is the HOSTING dimension that docs/research/infra-scaling-spike-2026-06.md and [[project_infra_scaling_spike]] deferred to second behind the job-queue/worker-pool work. The spike concluded I/O-bound (not CPU-bound), DB is the crux. This issue is narrower: just the billing-model migration (usage-based -> flat-rate). Does NOT include the GPU/RunPod classifier spend (that is #185 meter + #186 batching + #187 resilience) — RunPod stays separate regardless of where the web app runs.

DELIVERABLE: a short comparison (monthly $ at current scale, migration effort, ops burden of self-managed Postgres vs managed, TLS/domain story) + a recommendation. Then a migration plan if we proceed.

RESEARCH COMPLETE 2026-07-18 — recommendation: DECLINE the migration for now. Full writeup: docs/superpowers/plans/2026-07-18-scan-speedup-and-hosting.md

MONTHLY $ AT CURRENT SCALE — pulled live from the Railway API, not estimated. Production charcoal-web averages 0.0000 vCPU over 30 days (max 0.0005); prod Postgres 0.0001 vCPU; staging charcoal-web 0.0041 avg (max 0.68). RAM 0.04-0.10 GB per service. Priced at Railways published rates ($0.000463/vCPU-min, $0.000231/GB-min) both environments together meter at roughly $2-5/month — well inside the $20 usage that Pro already includes. The $30.32 May bill was a busy development month, not a structural floor. Confirm against the May invoice line items before treating this as settled.

DESTINATION GOT EXPENSIVE. Hetzner raised prices 15 June 2026 (official table: docs.hetzner.com/general/infrastructure-and-availability/price-adjustment/), very unevenly. CX/CAX rose ~30% but the dedicated-vCPU lines rose 2.2-2.7x: CCX33 62.49 -> 138.49 EUR, CCX43 124.99 -> 275.99 EUR, CPX42 25.49 -> 69.49 EUR. Driver is the DRAM price shock; netcup (+24%), Scaleway and OVH moved similarly. The Hetzner-is-much-cheaper framing is a pre-June-2026 artifact. Server Auction was explicitly EXEMPT from the increase and has no setup fee + unlimited traffic — the one genuinely cheap corner left.

COST TABLE (all-in, incl. backups/storage; two envs on one box): Railway today $30.32/mo, $364/yr. Hetzner CX53 (16 shared/32GB) ~$45.74/mo, $549/yr. netcup RS 4000 (12 DEDICATED EPYC/32GB) ~$42.75/mo, $513/yr but EU-only, no US DC. OVH RISE-S (8 dedicated Ryzen/64GB ECC, US DCs, unmetered) ~$80.75/mo + $77 setup, $969/yr. Vultr HP 8 ~$115/mo, $1380/yr. Every configuration with real dedicated cores costs MORE than Railway. A cheaper config exists (Hetzner CX43 ~$23/mo, saving ~$88/yr) but 8 shared vCPUs is less headroom than Railway will burst to.

OPS BURDEN / SELF-MANAGED POSTGRES. Hetzner has NO managed Postgres — verified against their own cloud product page and API changelog (note: there is AI-generated content on the web claiming otherwise; it is false). Choosing Hetzner IS choosing to self-manage Postgres. Realistic burden: 6-12h one-time setup, 1-3h/month steady state, 2-6h per major version upgrade every few years. pgvector 0.8.5 via the official pgvector/pgvector Docker images makes the extension a non-issue day-to-day; the cliff is major version upgrades (pg_upgrade needs both versions binaries, and the extension .so must exist for the NEW major before upgrading). At a few GB, dump-and-restore sidesteps most of that. Backups: skip PITR initially — pg_dump --format=custom every 6h to Backblaze B2 (~$0.30/mo, first 10GB free) plus a MONTHLY RESTORE REHEARSAL. Note pgBackRest was declared unmaintained 2026-04-27 then rescued 2026-05-18 by an AWS/Supabase/Percona/pgEdge/Tiger/Eon coalition — most blog posts about its death are stale.

TLS/DOMAIN STORY. Caddy solves it in ~4 lines with automatic Let's Encrypt + renewal; two subdomains need no wildcard so HTTP-01 suffices. Must mount Caddys /data as a named volume or every restart re-requests certs and hits rate limits. Highest-risk migration item is NOT TLS — it is AT Protocol OAuth: CHARCOAL_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID is a URL serving client metadata on the public hostname with exact-match redirect URIs, and CHARCOAL_SESSION_SECRET derives the stable P-256 signing key. Carry the secret across unchanged and verify the full OAuth round-trip on a temporary hostname BEFORE cutting DNS.

PaaS LAYER (if we ever proceed): Dokploy narrowly over Coolify — it is the only option with BOTH scheduled Postgres backups AND volume backups to S3 plus UI restore. Coolify has the bigger community but disclosed 11 critical CVEs in Jan 2026 and its maintainer publicly describes the codebase as under-tested. Avoid CapRover (no DB backups, crippled Compose). Build images in CI and deploy prebuilt — Rust builds will make a small box unresponsive.

THE DECISIVE POINT — this issue notes the infra spike concluded I/O-bound, not CPU-bound, DB is the crux. That holds and it cuts against migrating for performance. Separately: Railway meters ACTUAL consumption, so parallelism rearranges CPU-seconds rather than creating them — a 16-way scan costs the same CPU as a serial one and LESS RAM. Railways billing is not discouraging parallelization. And the scan is not core-starved anyway: the real serialization is a global Arc<Mutex<Session>> around all three ONNX models (toxicity/onnx.rs:45, topics/embeddings.rs:31, scoring/nli.rs:100), so only one inference per model runs process-wide regardless of gather concurrency. More cores do not widen that.

RECOMMENDATION: (1) close this as evaluated-declined; revisit if the bill sustains >$100/mo or user count grows. (2) The cheapest real lever today is staging idling 24/7 — sleep or tear it down between pushes. (3) If a flat-rate box is ever wanted it should host BATCH SCAN WORK, not charcoal.watch — note soot v2 already assumes a Hetzner box ~$80/mo, so that ops burden may be incurred regardless. That is an architecture change, not a migration.

CORRECTION to the PaaS recommendation in my earlier comment. A later research pass found that Dokploy — which I had recommended — published 11 security advisories on 2026-05-11, 8 of them critical. The worst (CVE-2026-45631, affects 0.27.0-0.28.8) is a pre-auth admin takeover: a hardcoded BETTER_AUTH_SECRET fallback that the install script never sets, so every default self-hosted instance shared one signing secret. Forge a JWT, auto-sign-in as admin, execute commands on the host via the built-in SSH terminal. Published PoC. Remediation required an out-of-band secret-rotation script that itself shipped with a bug. Additional risk: bus factor of one (maintainer has 4259 commits, the #2 human has 65; a second engineer starts ~now), a ~75% commit-rate slowdown since May 2026, an unresolved Traefik-on-Swarm bug class producing non-self-healing 502s, and 500-600MB idle RAM against a documented 2GB minimum that does not hold.

For an app holding AT Protocol OAuth tokens I no longer think any self-hosted PaaS control plane is the right call here. Coolify has the same shape of problem (11 critical flaws Jan 2026, seven at CVSS 10.0, ~52,890 exposed instances). CapRover is maintained but feature-frozen with no scheduled/S3 backups and no environment concept. Both leading options are Docker Swarm, which is the top failure mode in both issue trackers, and none of them take Postgres backups off your plate anyway. A PaaS adds an internet-facing, Docker-socket-holding admin panel — new attack surface layered on top of the ops burden it was meant to remove.

REVISED: if this migration ever happens, use plain Docker Compose + Caddy with no control plane. Caddy is also strictly more capable on TLS (DNS-01 and wildcards; kamal-proxy structurally cannot do either — verified, no ACME DNS library in its go.mod). Compose project names give cleaner prod/staging isolation than Kamal destinations, which have a documented service-name-collision hazard on a shared server. Build in CI and deploy prebuilt images either way. NOTE this does not change the top-line recommendation, which is still to DECLINE the migration — it only changes what we would do IF we proceeded.

issues.title: RunPod capacity resilience: multi-DC CoPE-B endpoints + GPU preference list + app-side endpoint failover
issues.description:
PROBLEM: The CoPE-B classifier endpoint (f39lf61pc762a8) is single-DC + effectively single-GPU. Its weights network volume (b8rmatu3si, "charcoal-cope-b-weights", 70GB) is pinned to US-GA-2, which is H100-only and capacity-volatile (#205). A RunPod network volume is bound to one data center, and attaching it constrains the endpoint to that DC — so even when A100 80GB SXM is "High" stock GLOBALLY (verified 2026-06-26 via list-gpu-types), the endpoint cannot use it, and the cheaper A100 (~$2.7/hr vs H100 ~$3.3-4.2/hr) is unreachable. Bryan confirmed live: no A100 available to this endpoint. This is the #205 single-region/single-arch failure mode.

GOAL: make CoPE-B serving resilient to both per-GPU and per-DC capacity dry-up, and unlock cheaper GPUs.

THREE LAYERS (do in order of leverage):
1. RELOCATE / DIVERSIFY DC: create a weights volume (~50GB, zentropi-ai/cope-b-a4b) in a DC with broad/cheap stock (A100 + RTX PRO 6000 96GB + L40S), not just US-GA-2. One-time: spin a cheap pod in the target DC, huggingface-cli download into the new volume, create an endpoint bound to it.
2. GPU PREFERENCE LIST per endpoint: allow multiple GPU types so RunPod picks cheapest-available — e.g. A100 80GB SXM (~$2.7) -> RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell 96GB (High stock, fits 50GB bf16, likely < H100) -> H100 80GB (fallback). Dodges per-GPU dry-up within a DC.
3. MULTI-DC ENDPOINTS + APP-SIDE FAILOVER: one volume+endpoint per DC (each ~$3.5/mo storage, compute scales to zero). App routes across them: try preferred/cheapest endpoint, fall back on capacity error/timeout. RunPod does NOT auto-LB across separate endpoints. Code: charcoal has a single RUNPOD_ENDPOINT_URL today — generalize to RUNPOD_ENDPOINT_URLS (ordered list) + try-in-order failover in RunPodCopeBClient, next to the existing CHARCOAL_CLASSIFIER=zentropi escape hatch. TDD the failover (first endpoint errors -> second used).

COST: storage only (N x ~50GB ~ $3.5/mo each); compute still scale-to-zero. Unlocks A100 (~20-35% cheaper than H100) immediately and removes the single-DC stranding.

Relates: #205 (capacity risk — this CLOSES that parked observation), #185 (honest cost meter — measures the savings), #186 (batching — orthogonal GPU-agnostic $1 lever), #196 (prod cost-guard), #207 (onboarding cost). Pairs with batching as the two-pronged path to <$1/onboarding.

comments.content: Field reality (Bryan 2026-06-26): A100 80GB serverless is capacity-constrained ACROSS RunPod, not just US-GA-2 — the list-gpu-types "High" label is aspirational; creating/running A100 endpoints struggles everywhere. PIVOT: do NOT target A100 as primary. (a) Realistic GPU-swap target = RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell 96GB ("High" stock, fits 50GB bf16, newer/cheaper-than-H100); A100 only opportunistic in the preference list; H200 141GB also "High" but pricier. (b) MORE IMPORTANT: batching (#186) is capacity-INDEPENDENT — runs on the existing H100, no new volume/DC, ~10-30x cut → the real path to $1 WITHOUT fighting A100 scarcity. SEQUENCE: #186 batching FIRST; #187 GPU/DC swap = bonus multiplier, not prerequisite. Bryan already creating volumes manually.

issues.title: Batch the RunPod classifier (one request -> many texts) to cut warm-idle GPU cost
issues.description:
Onboarding cost is dominated by warm-idle GPU time, not compute. The 2026-06-26 staging burst made 4421 classifier calls, each with RunPod executionTime ~130ms (single 0/1 token output, prefill-dominated). Actual GPU compute ≈ 4421×0.13s ≈ 9.6 GPU-min ≈ $0.53; real cost was ~$6-10 — ~90-95% warm-idle waste because the burst is queue-bound (delay ~3-4s vs compute ~0.13s) sending ONE text per /runsync request.

ROOT CAUSE: client sends one text per RunPod job; vLLM handler (gpu/cope-b-runpod/handler.py) already supports batching (max_num_seqs=32, AsyncLLMEngine continuous batching) but each serverless job carries a single text, so the batch capacity is unused.

FIX: send batches of N texts per /runsync request. Handler accepts a list[str], returns list[verdict]; client (src/toxicity/runpod_cope_b.rs) batches the burst queue into chunks of N (e.g. 32) and maps results back by post_uri. Cuts requests ~Nx, finishes the burst in minutes instead of ~36, slashing worker-hours toward the ~$0.53 compute floor.

EXPECTED: ~10-30x fewer RunPod requests; onboarding RunPod cost from ~$6-10 toward ~$0.50-1 (the $1/onboarding goal). Pairs with GPU right-sizing (deep-research in progress: A100 vs H100, quantization). Verify with the #185 honest cost meter post-deploy. TDD: handler batch test + client chunk/remap test; preserve per-row envelope (format_parent_reply) + verdict alignment by post_uri.

Relates: #185 (cost meter), #207 (onboarding speed/cost), #206 (cost backstop), #196 (prod cost-guard).

issues.title: Real-time real-cost accounting in ScanCostMeter (account for concurrent workers, not 1)
issues.description:
PROBLEM: ScanCostMeter (src/toxicity/cost_meter.rs) estimates spend as elapsed_secs/3600 * 329c/hr — a SINGLE-worker model. RunPod bills $3.29/hr PER worker (confirmed via get-endpoint costPerHr=3.29), and the burst deliberately runs up to workersMax=10 concurrently to drain the queue fast. So the meter undercounts real spend by ~the active-worker count (up to 10x). Live 2026-06-26 staging resume burst: app estimate ~$2.00 (squeaked under the $2 ceiling, 36m29s vs 36m28s trip threshold) while REAL RunPod cost was ~$6-10 (multiple H100 workers x $3.29/hr x 0.6hr). The $2 "cap" is not a real spend cap. (NOTE: workersMin=0 = scale-to-zero, no idle cost; workersStandby is FlashBoot warm-resume, not billed — earlier idle-cost worry was a misread.)

GOAL: meter computes real-time ACTUAL spend reflecting concurrent worker usage, so the disaster-brake trips on real dollars and we have an accurate running spend number.

DESIGN OPTIONS (decide):
(A) Conservative brake — multiply estimate by the effective worker count = min(burst_concurrency, workersMax), configurable via env (e.g. CHARCOAL_RUNPOD_WORKERS / read from config). With (16,10) => x10 => $2 trips at ~3.6min. Simplest, self-contained, no RunPod API calls. Worst-case (assumes saturation) — safe for a brake, slightly over-counts early/late burst.
(B) In-flight integral — meter tracks concurrently in-flight classify calls (counter inc on arm / dec on completion), integrates active_workers (capped at workersMax) over time x rate. Self-contained, more accurate (reflects ramp), real-time running spend. Moderate complexity.
(C) Poll RunPod /health for workers.running periodically and integrate actual worker-seconds. Most accurate (true billing proxy) but adds a RunPod API dependency + polling loop inside the scan.

RECOMMEND: (B) for accuracy without an external dependency, OR (A) if we just want a safe brake fast. Could ship (A) now + (B) as follow-up. Also: emit a periodic "estimated spend so far" INFO log during burst for observability regardless of which.

Relates to #183 (burst resilience, same chokepoint), #206 (the cost backstop this corrects), and the historical prod #196 cost-guard. TDD: pure over_ceiling/estimate tests with a worker-count factor; in-flight counter tests for (B).

issues.title: Make the amplification phase resumable (skip it when resuming burst/finalize)
issues.description:
PROBLEM: A resumed scan still re-runs the entire amplification phase (~65min for a big account) before it reaches the resumable part. #208 (classification burst decouple) made gather/burst/finalize restartable via the scan_phase marker inside run_phased_scan (src/pipeline/scan_phases/mod.rs) — but amplification::run (src/pipeline/amplification.rs) runs BEFORE run_phased_scan and is NOT gated on the marker. It re-collects Constellation events, re-scores amplifier NLI pairs, re-fetches all amplifier followers, and rebuilds the in-memory candidate set every single run. So when the original scan aborts/cost-caps in burst (scan_phase="burst", e.g. the #178/#183 run with 4905 pending) and is re-triggered, it pays the full amplification tax again just to get back to where run_phased_scan can skip gather. Observed live 2026-06-26: re-run started 15:42 UTC, spent ~65min in amplification before reaching burst, even though the 4905-row queue was already staged.

GOAL: When scan_phase indicates a resume (burst/finalize), skip amplification entirely (or short-circuit it) and go straight to run_phased_scan with the persisted candidate set + staged data — so a resume only does the work it actually left unfinished.

APPROACH (sketch, design first): persist the built candidate set (CandidateInput list + graph_distances/pile_on context) keyed by user_did alongside the existing scan_account_input staging, written at the point amplification hands off to run_phased_scan. On a run where scan_phase is burst/finalize, load the persisted candidates and skip amplification::run. On a fresh run (scan_phase None/Done/Gather), run amplification normally and (re)write the persisted candidates. Reconcile with the sweep path (run_sweep_phased) which has the same shape. Watch out: candidate sets can drift between runs (new events) — on resume we intentionally reuse the staged set, not rebuild. Also the amplification event-recording side effects (amplification_events table, last_scan_at) — decide whether those must run on resume.

VALUE: cuts resume wall-clock by ~the amplification duration (~65min on a big account); makes cost-cap / transient-interrupt (#183) resumes cheap instead of near-full-rescans. Related: #208 (phased scan), #207 (onboarding speed), #183 (burst now interruptible+resumable), #206 (cost-cap resumes). NOT urgent — resume is correct today, just expensive.

TDD: gate test for "scan_phase=burst => amplification skipped, candidates loaded from staging => run_phased_scan resumes burst"; fresh-run test for "scan_phase=None => amplification runs + persists candidates".

issues.title: Retry RunPod transport/connect errors in burst (backon predicate gap)
issues.description:
Steps to reproduce:
1. 

Expected: 
Actual: 

Live staging scan (2026-06-26, #178 validation) ABORTED in burst at 08:07:02 on a single: ERROR charcoal::web::scan_job: Pipeline error error=RunPod transport: error sending request for url (https://api.runpod.ai/v2/f39lf61pc762a8/runsync). One transient connect/transport blip killed a 4905-item burst. RunPodCopeBClient has backon retry (runpod_cope_b.rs ~367) but its .when() predicate / RetryClass likely does NOT classify a reqwest transport "error sending request" (connect/send failure, no HTTP status) as retryable, so it propagated as non-retryable → run_phased_scan Err → scan aborted. #208 makes it resumable, but a transient network blip should be retried, not abort. Part of the Phase 6 ship (#63 batch). Fix: classify reqwest transport/connect/timeout errors (is_connect/is_timeout/is_request, no status) as RETRYABLE with capped exponential backoff, alongside existing 5xx/429; keep genuine 4xx (non-429) + cost-cap non-retryable. TDD: unit test the retry-classification predicate for a transport error. Acceptance: a simulated transport error is retried (not aborted); 4xx still fails fast; cost-cap still non-retryable.

issues.title: Add 429/backoff retry to PublicAtpClient (Bluesky read client)
issues.description:
Current state: 

Desired state: 

Reason: 

PublicAtpClient::xrpc_get (src/bluesky/client.rs:65) bails on any non-2xx — including HTTP 429 rate-limit — with no retry or backoff. Surfaced by #178, which parallelized the second-degree sweep and amplification follower fetches (serial -> buffer_unordered, prod concurrency=8). Total request volume is unchanged vs serial, but the request RATE rises ~8x during the follower-crawl window, raising the chance of tripping Bluesky public appview rate limits.

Current failure mode is graceful (a 429 -> fetch_followers Err -> follower warn-logged and skipped -> scan finishes degraded, re-runnable), so this is hardening, not a blocker. But dropped coverage on rate-limited fetches degrades scan completeness.

Scope: wrap xrpc_get (and likely resolve_pds_url) request execution in exponential backoff that retries on 429 + 5xx + transient network errors, honoring Retry-After when present; leave 4xx (except 429) as hard failures. Mirror the existing backon-based pattern in src/toxicity/runpod_cope_b.rs:367 (ExponentialBuilder + Retryable + .when() filter) rather than zentropi.rs hand-rolled sleep loop. backon = "1" is already a dependency.

Trigger to prioritize: if the #178 staging validation scan shows 429s in logs, do this before staging->main promotion. If the scan is clean at concurrency=8, lower urgency.

Acceptance: 429/5xx/network errors retried with capped exponential backoff; non-retryable 4xx still fail fast; unit test for the retry-classification predicate.

The problem: context_multiplier range is 1.0-1.5x, so a Low-tier account with raw_score 3.0 and extreme context (1.0) only gets boosted to 4.5 — still Low. Someone who is specifically hostile to Bryan's content but generally non-toxic flies under the radar.

Options to evaluate:
1. Widen multiplier range (1.0-2.0x or 1.0-2.5x) — simple, keeps multiplicative property
2. Add context as independent signal (e.g. if context_score > 0.5, floor raw_score at Watch threshold) — catches concern trolls but risks false positives
3. Hybrid: use higher multiplier AND lower the Watch threshold when context_score is high
4. Keep as-is — the current design correctly says 'low general toxicity = low threat even with hostile interactions'

Key question for Bryan: is someone who is ONLY hostile toward your content but polite everywhere else a threat worth flagging? If yes, option 2 or 3. If no, option 4 is correct.

Graph distance scoring now implemented and deployed to staging. The context_multiplier (1.0-1.5x) is augmented by graph_distance weight (0.6-1.2x). Strangers with hostile context scores now get a meaningful boost. Need to re-evaluate tier distribution after staging scan completes to see if the combined effect of context_multiplier * graph_distance_weight is sufficient to promote accounts across tier boundaries.

comments.content:
Code review findings from PR #6 (SQLite-to-PostgreSQL). Fix before merge.

CRITICAL:
1. Migration race condition: run_migrations() has no transaction wrapping. Partial failure leaves DB corrupt. Wrap each migration in BEGIN/COMMIT. Note: CREATE EXTENSION cannot run in a transaction — handle migration 1 specially.
2. Fragile interval in is_score_stale: uses string concat ($2 || ' days'). Replace with make_interval(days => $2) and bind i32 directly.

HIGH:
3. status::show breaks for Postgres: checks SQLite file existence, prints 'not initialized' when no .db file exists. Need backend-aware status display.
4. u32::MAX to i32 overflow: migrate command calls get_recent_events(u32::MAX), cast to i32 wraps to -1. Cap at i32::MAX or add get_all_events method.
5. Migration only copies 2 hardcoded scan_state keys: add get_all_scan_state() to trait or query SQLite directly.
6. Migration loses event timestamps/IDs: insert_amplification_event sets detected_at=NOW(). All migrated events get same timestamp, breaking pile-on detection. Need raw INSERT preserving original timestamps.

MEDIUM (fix later):
7. save_embedding UPDATE silently does nothing if no fingerprint row exists
8. No concurrent migration protection (advisory locks)
9. CREATE EXTENSION vector needs superuser (document requirement)
10. Postgres tests dont clean up (consider sqlx::test or transaction rollback)
11. post_count as i32 could overflow
12. sqlite feature flag defined but never gated

issues.description:
Detect amplification attacks where users screenshot posts instead of quoting/reposting them. This bypasses Constellation and AT Protocol backlinks entirely.

**Phase 1: Text-based search (searchPosts)**
Use app.bsky.feed.searchPosts to find posts mentioning the protected user's handle as plain text (not @mentions). Attackers who screenshot posts almost always reference the handle in their commentary. Low-cost, catches the majority of cases. Known example: @aly.codes was screenshotting posts for toxic amplification.

**Phase 2: Image OCR (production-informed)**
For high-signal situations learned from production usage, add OCR scanning of attached images to detect:
- Protected user's handle visible in screenshots
- Protected user's post text matched in screenshot content
Consider local ONNX OCR model (tesseract or similar) to stay consistent with the local-first approach. Only activate for situations where Phase 1 misses attacks that are confirmed through other channels.

**Notes:**
- Phase 2 scope should be shaped by production data — what patterns do screenshot attackers actually follow?
- OCR is expensive; may want to limit to posts from accounts already flagged or from known hostile amplification chains
- Deciduous nodes: 100 (goal), 101 (decision)

Recalibrate threat scoring for sentence embedding overlap scale

comments.content:
Problem: With sentence embeddings, topic overlap values are 0.45-0.85 for most accounts in the social neighborhood, vs 0.05-0.24 with TF-IDF. The current formula (toxicity*70 + overlap*30) gives scores of 15-25 from overlap alone, putting nearly everyone at Elevated or higher regardless of toxicity.

Real data from fresh scan: 12 High, 160 Elevated, 32 Watch, 9 Low out of 213 accounts. Most 'High' accounts have toxicity <0.10 — they're allies.

Fix approach: 
1. Change formula so toxicity is the PRIMARY driver and overlap is a multiplier/modifier rather than additive
2. New formula: score = toxicity_component * (1 + overlap_boost). High overlap with low toxicity = low score. High overlap WITH toxicity = amplified score.
3. Raise tier thresholds to reflect real-world score distributions
4. Keep the overlap gate (below threshold = capped) but adjust threshold for embedding scale
5. Update all unit tests and composition tests to match new formula

issues.description:
Replace notification-based amplification detection with Constellation's public graph API. This enables scanning any public user without their credentials.

Key requirements:
- Constellation API for detecting quote/repost events on any user's posts
- protected_users table: list of users we actively monitor (manual add or signup)
- Per-user topic fingerprints (each protected user has their own)
- Shared toxicity scores across all protected users (scored once, reused)
- Threat scores combine shared toxicity with per-user topic overlap
- Users should not be added to protection until they sign up or are manually added

Scaling & accuracy:
- Tiered scoring depth: broad shallow pass (20 posts), deepen Watch-tier to 100+
- Background job for new user onboarding (build fingerprint, then sweep network async)
- Higher concurrency for network-bound work (16-32 vs current 8)
- Shared toxicity is the biggest scaling win — score once, compute overlap per-user cheaply

Fingerprint & score lifecycle:
- Full fingerprints for protected users and targets, refreshed periodically with new posts only (incremental, not full rebuild)
- Toxicity scores refresh the same way — add new post scores incrementally
- CRITICAL: never overwrite scores that a user has already acted on (blocked, muted, reviewed). Historical basis for actions must be preserved. Score history or versioning needed.
- Consider: action_log table linking user actions to the score snapshot that informed them

Some of this may be v2 (score versioning, incremental refresh) vs v1 (Constellation + multi-user + protected_users table).

Supersedes #19 (configurable protected user).
See docs/research-alternative-approaches.md for Constellation discussion.

issues.title: Select and implement Perspective API replacement

comments.content: Research complete (issue #11). Full report at docs/toxicity-alternatives-report.md. Top recommendation: Detoxify unbiased-toxic-roberta via ONNX Runtime (ort crate). Backup: OpenAI Moderation API. Future complement: CoPE by Zentropi for policy-steerable edge cases. This issue blocks scaling the MVP since Perspective API has a 1 QPS rate limit and sunsets Dec 31, 2026.

DECISION POINTS:

1. Primary scorer: Detoxify unbiased-toxic-roberta via ONNX (recommended) vs alternatives
   - Returns continuous 0-1 scores across 7 categories (matches existing ToxicityScorer trait)
   - Identity-bias mitigation (critical for Bryan's topic areas: fat liberation, queer identity, DEI)
   - Pre-exported ONNX at protectai/unbiased-toxic-roberta-onnx (~125 MB quantized)
   - Runs on CPU, no API dependency, Apache 2.0
   - Alternative: MiniLMv2-toxic-jigsaw-onnx (22M params, even smaller, ROC-AUC 0.98)

2. Integration approach: ort crate (pure Rust ONNX Runtime bindings) recommended
   - Production-proven (SurrealDB, Bloop, Google Magika)
   - Pair with tokenizers crate for text tokenization
   - Alternative: rust-bert (adds ~2GB libtorch dep), candle (pure Rust but more manual work)

3. Fallback strategy: OpenAI Moderation API as cloud backup
   - Free, 13 harm categories, 95% accuracy, easy reqwest integration
   - Useful when local inference isn't available or for cross-validation

4. Timeline: Replace now vs keep Perspective and swap later
   - Perspective works until Dec 31, 2026, but has 1 QPS rate limit blocking pipeline scaling
   - Local ONNX scorer eliminates rate limits entirely
   - Replacing now unblocks issues #9 (rate limiting) and #10 (pipeline scaling)

RELATED ISSUES:
- #9 (rate limiting) becomes moot with local scorer
- #10 (pipeline scaling) partially unblocked by removing API rate limits
- #11 (research) completed, report at docs/toxicity-alternatives-report.md
